items.front: When may a side-step maneuver be used?
items.back: When an instrument approach procedure authorizes clearing the aircraft to one runway while landing is made on a parallel runway.

items.front: What runway relationship defines the side-step maneuver?
items.back: The aircraft is cleared to one runway and lands on a parallel runway.

items.front: What phraseology may be used to authorize the side-step?
items.back: Cleared ILS Runway Seven Left approach. Side-step to Runway Seven Right.

items.front: What minima consideration applies to side-step maneuvers?
items.back: They require higher published weather minima or MDA.

items.front: Where are the higher side-step minima or MDA published?
items.back: On the instrument approach chart.

items.front: When should a change to the advisory service frequency be approved for an IFR aircraft landing at an airport not served by a tower or FSS?
items.back: When direct communications are no longer required.

items.front: What phraseology releases the aircraft to the local advisory frequency?
items.back: Change to advisory frequency approved.

items.front: What airport type does this communications-release rule address?
items.back: An airport not served by a control tower or FSS.

items.front: Why is an expeditious frequency change useful here?
items.back: It permits the aircraft to receive timely local airport traffic information.

items.front: Except for what type of aircraft does an approach clearance automatically authorize the depicted missed approach?
items.back: Except for a VFR aircraft practicing an instrument approach.

items.front: If an alternate missed approach is necessary, what must ATC issue?
items.back: The alternate missed approach description as published on the appropriate FAA or military form for the IAP.

items.front: When may the alternate missed approach description be omitted?
items.back: When the alternate missed approach is published and in effect via NOTAM.

items.front: After an aircraft commences the missed approach, where may it be vectored?
items.back: At or above the MVA or MIA, or under the applicable vectors-below-minimum-altitude provisions.

items.front: Unless otherwise cleared, when does the pilot begin a missed-approach turn?
items.back: At the missed approach point.

items.front: When the pilot says they are unfamiliar with the procedure, what initial information must be specified?
items.back: The initial approach altitude.

items.front: What procedure-turn information must be specified to an unfamiliar pilot?
items.back: The direction and distance from the holding fix within which the procedure turn is to be completed, and the altitude at which the procedure turn is to be made.

items.front: What final-approach information must be specified to an unfamiliar pilot?
items.back: The final approach course and altitude.

items.front: When may missed approach procedures be specified for an unfamiliar pilot?
items.back: If considered necessary.

items.front: What chart notations should be called out to an unfamiliar pilot?
items.back: Applicable approach-chart notations that place responsibility on the pilot to comply with a published limitation or instruction.

items.front: What phraseology introduces the unfamiliar-pilot approach briefing?
items.back: Initial approach at (altitude), procedure turn at (altitude), (number) minutes or miles (direction), final approach on (name of NAVAID) (specified) course, radial, or azimuth at (altitude).

items.front: If workload or traffic prevents a practice instrument approach, what should ATC tell the pilot?
items.back: The reason and, if applicable, the anticipated delay until the approach can be approved.

items.front: Should controller-imposed delays for practice approaches be indefinite?
items.back: No.

items.front: What general traffic-flow limitation applies to practice instrument approaches?
items.back: Except for military aircraft at military airfields, they must not disrupt the flow of other arriving and departing IFR or VFR aircraft.

items.front: What must a VFR aircraft practicing an instrument approach be told on initial contact or as soon as possible thereafter?
items.back: Maintain VFR.

items.front: What phraseology is used when a VFR practice approach is approved with no separation services provided?
items.back: Maintain VFR, practice approach approved, no separation services provided.

items.front: Is a VFR aircraft automatically authorized to execute the missed approach during a practice instrument approach?
items.back: No. It must be specifically requested by the pilot and approved by the controller.

items.front: When a facility directive requires IFR separation for a VFR practice approach, how long does that separation continue if the missed approach is approved?
items.back: Throughout the procedure, including the missed approach.

items.front: Before touchdown or crossing the landing threshold, how is an aircraft on a touch-and-go, low approach, or practice approach treated?
items.back: As an arriving aircraft.

items.front: After touchdown or threshold crossing, how is that aircraft treated?
items.back: As a departing aircraft.

items.front: When must departure instructions for a low approach or touch-and-go be issued?
items.back: Before the aircraft begins final descent.

items.front: What must climb-out instructions include after a low approach or touch-and-go?
items.back: A specific heading or a route of flight and altitude, except when the aircraft will maintain VFR and contact the tower.

items.front: What phraseology may be used for a low-approach climb-out to 6,000 and right heading 360?
items.back: After completing low approach, climb and maintain six thousand. Turn right, heading three six zero.

items.front: When may climb-out instructions be omitted after the first approach?
items.back: When the instructions remain the same.
